turns.content: I just got my code back together after five years, but our CI/CD pipeline keeps failing because rustdoc can't find the examples in my module. I added /// doc comments with inline code blocks as tests using #[test] attributes, but it seems like the parser is ignoring them or treating them as regular text instead of executable tests. I have a working solution that passes locally by manually running cargo test on specific files, but does there exist a better way to ensure these doc examples are actually run during the build process?

The issue you're facing stems from how rustdoc handles code within /// doc comments versus actual #[test] functions. When you place executable Rust code inside doc strings without proper formatting or when those lines aren't recognized as tests by the compiler, they get skipped entirely during documentation generation and test execution. Think of it like trying to drive a car with your engine disconnected from the transmission—you have all the parts in place but nothing is actually connected to make the motion happen. Your current approach works locally because cargo test explicitly invokes those functions, but rustdoc doesn't automatically parse doc comments as tests unless they follow specific conventions that trigger the doc-test feature. The better way is to ensure your /// doc examples are placed within code blocks formatted exactly like #[test] attributes and verify you're using the right flags when invoking rustdoc or cargo test. Many developers mistakenly believe that any Rust code appearing in documentation will be tested automatically, but this isn't true unless explicitly configured through proper syntax and tooling setup.

That makes sense about the engine-transmission analogy, so I think I understand why my tests aren't running during docs generation. But wait, I thought doc comments with /// were supposed to include inline code blocks that get executed automatically? Isn't that how it's documented in the standard library? I added #[test] attributes directly inside those comment lines expecting them to be picked up without extra configuration.

You've hit a common misconception where developers assume documentation syntax guarantees test execution, but rustdoc only recognizes code as tests when properly structured within code blocks and formatted according to specific rules. The standard library examples do get tested because they follow the exact pattern required for doc-tests, not just because they're inside /// comments. When you place #[test] attributes directly in doc strings without wrapping them in proper code formatting or ensuring the right flags are set during your build process, rustdoc treats those lines as regular text rather than executable test cases. This is why your local cargo test runs but rustdoc fails to execute them—the tooling needs explicit instructions to recognize and run those examples. You can fix this by restructuring how you define your doc comments so they align with the expected format for automatic test execution.

Okay, I see now that it's about proper formatting rather than just having #[test] attributes in /// lines. Can you give me a concrete example of what the correct syntax looks like versus my incorrect version? And once fixed, how do I verify these doc tests are actually running during the build without manually checking each file?

Here's the difference: your incorrect version might look like '/// This is a test #[test] fn foo() { ... }' where the attributes sit loosely in plain text. The correct format wraps everything in code blocks and ensures rustdoc recognizes the structure as executable tests, often requiring flags like --document-private-items or specific invocation parameters to trigger proper parsing. Once fixed, you can verify these doc tests are running by checking the output of 'cargo test' which will now include results from your /// examples alongside regular unit tests. You don't need separate verification steps if the syntax is right because cargo integrates doc-tests automatically once properly configured, so they run together with your normal test suite during every build cycle.

I'm trying to add doc comments to my new module but rustdoc is ignoring them because they're on the same line as the impl block. I used /// before the struct but it's not being parsed correctly, and even if I put them above, the docs don't show up in the generated HTML output.

That happens because rustdoc only captures doc comments on lines that are completely empty of other code or appear as separate physical lines. You need to place /// before the type definition on its own line so the parser can recognize it as a documentation comment rather than part of the source code logic.

Okay, I got that. But then I tried adding doc examples inside the module using # [test] but rustdoc is treating them as regular code instead of executable tests. How do I make those actually run during cargo test?

You need to use the #[cfg_attr(doc, doc_tests::cfg)] attribute or simply rely on standard doc comments with #[test] blocks that rustdoc will automatically execute. Actually, you should just write normal #[test] functions alongside your code and let cargo test run them without needing special cfg attributes for basic tests.
